10.1016/j.jmp.2022.102710
No data was used for the research described in the article.

Stochastic choice behavior is customarily modeled as a  system of choice probabilities  ( Falmagne, 1978 ) specifying, for every subset  
                         S 
                       of a relevant universe of items  
                         N 
                       and every item  
                         i 
                       in  
                         S 
                      , the probability that the decision maker chooses  
                         i 
                       from  
                         S 
                      . Definitions of stochastic rationality are generally expressed as properties of such systems. But choice data may be sparse; they often take the form of a system of  binary  probabilities recording, for every pair of items  
                         
                            i 
                            , 
                            j 
                         
                       in  
                         N 
                      , the frequency  
                         
                            
                               a 
                            
                            
                               i 
                               j 
                            
                         
                       with which the decision maker selects  
                         i 
                       from  
                         
                            
                               i 
                               , 
                               j 
                            
                         
                      . This raises the issue of determining the conditions under which a system of binary probabilities is induced by (or, phrased differently, can be extended to) a rational system of choice probabilities.

The answer obviously depends on what is meant by rationality. One natural interpretation, proposed by  Block and Marschak (1960) , is that there exists a probability distribution over the linear orderings on  
                         N 
                       such that the probability that  
                         i 
                       is the best item in  
                         S 
                       (for a linear ordering drawn at random according to that distribution) coincides with the probability that  
                         i 
                       is chosen from  
                         S 
                      . If that is the case, we say that the system of choice probabilities is  rationalizable by a random ordering.  The restrictions implied by this assumption on the binary probabilities are notoriously difficult to identify: see  Fishburn (1992)  for an early survey of the literature, and  Marley and Regenwetter (2017)  or  Martí and Reinelt (2011)  for an account of recent advances. The problem, known as the linear ordering polytope problem, remains open for a universal set containing more than  
                         7 
                       items.

In this note, we focus on a weaker but compelling condition of rationality known as  regularity , which stipulates that the probability of selecting an item from a set should not increase when that set expands.  Dasgupta and Pattanaik (2007)  argue that “given its weakness, transparency, and intuitive appeal, regularity recommends itself as the natural axiomatic foundation for the theory of choice behavior”.

Regularity is known to imply the  triangle inequalities  
                      
                         
                            
                               
                                  a 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                                  j 
                               
                            
                            + 
                            
                               
                                  a 
                               
                               
                                  j 
                                  k 
                               
                            
                            + 
                            
                               
                                  a 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                                  i 
                               
                            
                            ≥ 
                            1 
                         
                       for all three distinct  
                         
                            i 
                            , 
                            j 
                            , 
                            k 
                            ∈ 
                            N 
                         
                      . Indeed, if  
                         
                            
                               
                                  a 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                                  j 
                               
                            
                            + 
                            
                               
                                  a 
                               
                               
                                  j 
                                  k 
                               
                            
                            + 
                            
                               
                                  a 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                                  i 
                               
                            
                            < 
                            1 
                         
                      , regularity entails that the sum of the probabilities of choosing  
                         
                            i 
                            , 
                            j 
                            , 
                            k 
                         
                       from the set  
                         
                            
                               i 
                               , 
                               j 
                               , 
                               k 
                            
                         
                       is smaller than  
                         1 
                      , which is impossible. The purpose of this note is to prove that no further restriction on the binary probabilities is implied: every system of binary probabilities satisfying the triangle inequalities can be extended to a regular system of choice probabilities. 
                         2 
                      
                      
                         2 
                         Of course, as  Luce and Suppes (1965)  note, not every extension of a system of binary probabilities satisfying the triangle inequalities is regular.

Some background information about the triangle inequalities may be useful. It appears that they were first discussed by  Guilbaud (1953) . While  Marschak (1960)  conjectured that every system of binary probabilities satisfying these inequalities is rationalizable by a random ordering,  Dridi (1980)  was the first to publish a proof that this is true when the universal set contains no more than  
                         5 
                       items, but false when it contains at least  
                         6 
                       items. Complementary historical information may be found in  Marley (1990)  and  Fishburn (1992) . Experimental evidence supporting the triangle inequalities is reported in  Regenwetter, Dana, and Davis-Stober (2011) .

To avoid any risk of confusion, let us emphasize that the theorem we prove in the current paper is about regularity, not about rationalizability by a random ordering. It bears repeating that the triangle inequalities are  not  sufficient to ensure that a system of binary probabilities is rationalizable by a random ordering.

Theorem 
                         
                            A regular extension of  
                            
                               a 
                             
                            exists if and only if  
                            
                               a 
                             
                            satisfies the triangle inequalities.

A regular extension of  
                            
                               a 
                             
                            exists if and only if  
                            
                               a 
                             
                            satisfies the triangle inequalities.

Since the “only if” statement is well known, we only prove the “if” statement. A direct, constructive proof appears to be difficult. Note in particular that the naive construction  
                         
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        x 
                                     
                                     
                                        i 
                                     
                                     
                                        S 
                                     
                                  
                                  =

                                              ∈ 
                                              S 
                                              ∖ 
                                              i 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           
                                              a 
                                           
                                           
                                              i 
                                              j

                                                       S 
                                                       | 
                                                    
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    2 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                      does not generally define a regular extension of  
                         a 
                      . For instance, suppose  
                         
                            N 
                            =

                                  =

                       from

Our proof of the theorem stated in Section  2  is nonconstructive. Since conditions  (2) ,  (3) ,  (4) ,  (5)  are linear inequalities on  
                         x 
                      , the theorem of the alternative can be used to derive linear inequalities on  
                         a 
                       that are necessary and sufficient for the existence of a regular extension  
                         x 
                       of  
                         a 
                      . It turns out that the coefficients in these inequalities can be interpreted as the parameters of suitably defined systems of flows defined over  
                         
                            
                               N 
                            
                            ¯ 
                         
                      . We show that these systems of flows are spanned by the systems whose characteristics generate the triangle inequalities. The latter are therefore necessary and sufficient for the existence of a regular extension of a system of binary probabilities.

Our analysis makes use of concepts borrowed from the theory of network flows. While the exposition is self-contained, the reader may find it profitable to consult  [3]  or  [1]  for complementary information on network flows.

Before applying the theorem of the alternative, we make a preliminary observation. The  cyclic inequalities  generated by a system of binaries  
                         a 
                       are the inequalities

                                        a 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              i 
                                           
                                           
                                              2 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           
                                              i 
                                           
                                           
                                              3 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  + 
                                  ⋯ 
                                  + 
                                    
                                  
                                     
                                        a 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              i 
                                           
                                           
                                              m 
                                              − 
                                              1

                                  1 
                                  , 
                               
                            
                         
                      where

Proof 
                         This is a consequence of the theorem of the alternative. Consider the system of inequalities  (2)  to ( (5)  ).  ■

This is a consequence of the theorem of the alternative. Consider the system of inequalities  (2)  to ( (5)  ).  ■

Lemma 3 
                         
                            The characteristic inequality of a simple decreasing flow is implied by the triangle inequalities.

The characteristic inequality of a simple decreasing flow is implied by the triangle inequalities.

Our next step consists in generalizing  Lemma 3  to all decreasing flows. To this end, we first show that every decreasing flow is the sum of a collection of simple decreasing flows.

                            .

Proof 
                         Let  
                               F 
                             be a decreasing flow. Rank the sets  
                               
                                  S 
                                  ∈ 
                                  N 
                               
                             in a  size-increasing  sequence  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        
                                           
                                              S 
                                           
                                           
                                              t 
                                           
                                        
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     t 
                                     = 
                                     1 
                                     , 
                                     … 
                                     , 
                                     T

                             is a simple decreasing flow; thus

                                     T

                               T

                               G 
                            , which is  Lemma 4 .

The proof is by induction. Fix

                                  = 
                                  1

                            ■

Lemmas 5 ,  6 ,  7  together imply that the characteristic inequality of every balanced flow is implied by the triangle inequalities and the trivial inequalities. Since the later are part of the definition of a system of binaries, the proof of the theorem is complete.

(1) Regular systems of choice probabilities were studied by  Dasgupta and Pattanaik (2007)  and  Suck (2016) , but neither of these papers is concerned with the issue of identifying the restrictions implied by regularity on the binary probabilities.

Dasgupta and Pattanaik (2007)  analyze the connection between regularity and the so-called weak axiom of stochastic revealed preference (WASRP) introduced by  Bandyopadhyay, Dasgupta, and Pattanaik (1999) . A system of choice probabilities  
                         x 
                       satisfies WASRP if and only if  
                         
                            
                               
                                  ∑ 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                                  ∈ 
                                  
                                     
                                        S 
                                     
                                     
                                        ″ 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                            
                               
                                  x 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        S 
                                     
                                     
                                        ′ 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                            − 
                            
                               
                                  ∑ 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                                  ∈ 
                                  
                                     
                                        S 
                                     
                                     
                                        ″ 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                            
                               
                                  x 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                               
                                  S 
                               
                            
                            ≤ 
                            
                               
                                  ∑ 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                                  ∈ 
                                  S 
                                  ∖ 
                                  
                                     
                                        S 
                                     
                                     
                                        ′ 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                            
                               
                                  x 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                               
                                  S 
                               
                            
                         
                       for all  
                         
                            S 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  S 
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  S 
                               
                               
                                  ″ 
                               
                            
                            ∈ 
                            
                               
                                  N 
                               
                               ¯ 
                            
                         
                       such that  
                         
                            
                               
                                  S 
                               
                               
                                  ″ 
                               
                            
                            ⊆ 
                            S 
                            ∩ 
                            
                               
                                  S 
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                         
                      .  Dasgupta and Pattanaik (2007)  show that Regularity is equivalent to WASRP. However, an incomplete system of choice probabilities (in which not all choice probabilities are observed), may violate WASRP while satisfying regularity.

Suck (2016)  derives conditions under which a regular incomplete system of choice probabilities is rationalizable by a random ordering. The conditions are variants of the Block–Marschak inequalities. Suck discusses an example where the choice probabilities  
                         
                            
                               x 
                            
                            
                               i 
                            
                            
                               S 
                            
                         
                       are only observed for the sets  
                         
                            S 
                            = 
                            
                               
                                  1 
                                  , 
                                  2 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  1 
                                  , 
                                  2 
                                  , 
                                  3 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            … 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  1 
                                  , 
                                  2 
                                  , 
                                  … 
                                  , 
                                  n 
                               
                            
                         
                      . In that case, rationalizability by a random ordering is equivalent to regularity and nonnegativity.

(2) The idea of applying flow network techniques to the study of stochastic choice behavior is not new.  Fiorini (2004)  uses such techniques to provide a short proof of  Falmagne (1978) ’s characterization of the systems of choice probabilities that are rationalizable by a random ordering.

Fiorini’s proof relies on a fundamental result in network theory establishing an equivalence between the set of flows from a source to a sink of a network and the convex hull of the directed paths linking the source to the sink. Formally, let  
                         
                            D 
                            = 
                            
                               ( 
                               V

                               A

                         A 
                      , fix two distinguished nodes  
                         
                            v 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  v 
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                            ∈ 
                            V 
                         
                      , and assume that each arc is contained in some  
                         
                            v 
                            − 
                            
                               
                                  v 
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                         
                       dipath. Any  
                         
                            v 
                            − 
                            
                               
                                  v 
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                         
                       dipath can be viewed as a vector in  
                         
                            
                               
                                  
                                     0 
                                     , 
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                            
                            
                               A 
                            
                         
                      , and the convex hull of these dipaths is called the  
                         
                            v 
                            − 
                            
                               
                                  v 
                               
                               
                                  ′

We showed that the triangle inequalities are necessary and sufficient to extend a system of binaries  
                         a 
                       to a  regular  system of choice probabilities. The first step of our argument uses the theorem of the alternative. The latter is directly applicable because regularity amounts to a collection of linear inequalities on  
                         a 
                      , namely,  (5) .

In principle, the theorem of the alternative could also be applied, albeit indirectly, to the linear ordering polytope problem, i.e., the problem of finding minimal necessary and sufficient conditions for extending  
                         a 
                       to a system of choice probabilities  
                         x 
                       that is  rationalizable by an ordering.  In contrast to regularity, rationalizability is not a system of linear inequalities per se. But Falmagne’s theorem asserts that it is equivalent to such a system:  
                         x 
                       is rationalizable by a random ordering if and only if it satisfies the Block–Marschak conditions  
                         
                            (32) 
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        ∑ 
                                     
                                     
                                        T 
                                        : 
                                          
                                        S 
                                        ⊆ 
                                        T 
                                        ⊆ 
                                        N 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           − 
                                           1 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           | 
                                           T 
                                           ∖ 
                                           S 
                                           | 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                    
                                  
                                     
                                        x 
                                     
                                     
                                        i 
                                     
                                     
                                        T 
                                     
                                  
                                  ≥ 
                                  0 
                                   for all  
                                  S 
                                  ∈ 
                                  
                                     
                                        N 
                                     
                                     ¯ 
                                  
                                   and  
                                  i 
                                  ∈ 
                                  S 
                                  . 
                               
                            
                         
                      Thus, the linear ordering problem amounts to finding necessary and sufficient conditions for the existence of a vector  
                         
                            x 
                            ∈ 
                            X 
                         
                       satisfying  (2) ,  (3) ,  (4) , and  (32) . This is a linear system. The theorem of the alternative applies again and delivers a set of inequalities on  
                         a 
                      . The vectors of the coefficients of these inequalities form a convex polytope whose extreme points generate facet-defining inequalities of the linear ordering polytope. This approach might help identify new facets of that polytope.
